Developer Guide: Fusion Runtime Core v3.01. Architecture InternalsThe heart of Supernova is the FusionExecutor. It does NOT use Mio or Tokio. It uses a custom PollingStrategy trait.1.1 The Task LayoutA FusionTask is a fat pointer containing:Future: The state machine.Context: Tier level (Native/WASM) and Priority.Affinity: Device ID (CPU core, GPU stream, QPU ID).1.2 The Hyper-Ring (Reactor)Located in src/reactor/ring.rs.The reactor maintains separate queues for different interrupt sources to avoid lock contention:network_cq: For TCP/UDP/RDMA packets.device_cq: Mapped to CUDA/ROCm event streams.timer_heap: A binary heap for sleep operations.When Runtime::block_on is called, the main thread enters a loop:Check local_queue for runnable tasks.Steal from global_queue if empty.Poll device_cq (non-blocking).Poll network_cq (non-blocking).If no work found, enter driver.park() (sleep state) or busy-spin depending on config.2. Building the RuntimeRequirementsRust: Nightly (for GATs and unstable async features).LLVM: 14+ (for WASM JIT).CUDA Toolkit: 11.0+ (if GPU feature enabled).Build Commands# Full build with all heterogeneous features
cargo build --release --features "full"

# Build strictly for edge devices (no heavy GPU support)
cargo build --release --no-default-features --features "wasm,distributed"
3. Developing ExtensionsTo add a new Backend (e.g., a proprietary FPGA):Implement DeviceBackend trait.Implement AsyncKernel trait for the device.Register the backend in RuntimeBuilder.impl DeviceBackend for MyFpga {
    fn submit(&self, job: Job) -> TaskId {
        // Write to FPGA ring buffer
    }
    
    fn poll(&self) -> Option<CompletedJob> {
        // Check MMIO register
    }
}
4. CI/CD & TestingWe use a specialized test harness fusion-test that mocks GPUs and QPUs for CI environments where hardware isn't available.# Run standard tests
cargo test

# Run simulation tests (Mocks hardware latency)
cargo test --features "simulation"
